title: Money Pulse · 2026-07-05
intro: What journalists, analysts, and people on X are saying about money this week — summarized by our desk with links to originals.

sections.topic: stock market and index investing this week
sections.headline: Wall Street Closes Bumper Quarter Amid AI Rotation Anxiety and Geopolitical Headwinds
sections.summary: Major U.S. indexes delivered their strongest quarterly performance in years despite a holiday-shortened week marked by renewed skepticism about AI valuations and Middle East tensions. The Dow Jones hit fresh highs while the Nasdaq retreated below key technical levels, highlighting a divergence as investors hunt for the next wave of AI winners beyond chip makers.
sections.editorial:
The market's schizophrenic behavior this week encapsulates a broader transition underway in equity markets. While [Wall Street concluded one of its strongest quarters in years](https://www.reuters.com/business/us-stock-futures-little-changed-strong-quarter-nears-end-2026-06-30/), the celebration felt muted as the [Nasdaq fell back below its 21-day and 50-day moving averages](https://www.investors.com/market-trend/stock-market-today/dow-jones-futures-ai-tumbles-apple-robinhood-buy-signals-tesla-sandisk-dive/) on Thursday. This technical weakness reflects [growing anxiety about AI's potential losers](https://www.reuters.com/commentary/reuters-open-interest/youth-task-forces-ai-debt-notes-week-2026-07-01/), particularly in the software-as-a-service sector where rising borrowing premia signal stress. The hunt for [AI's next winners beyond chipmakers](https://www.cnbc.com/2026/07/03/the-hunt-for-ais-next-winners-defined-the-stock-markets-holiday-shortened-week.html) is driving capital into unexpected corners, with FedEx emerging as a beneficiary through AI-related data center logistics demand.

Beneath the headline index gains, sector rotation accelerated dramatically. Semiconductor stocks faced severe pressure, with SanDisk plunging 14% and KLA Corporation dropping 12%, while healthcare and industrial names outperformed. The technical picture [showed 65% of key metrics flashing oversold conditions](https://x.com/DanielTNiles/status/1910166280654455153) during the recent selloff, above the historical 48% threshold typically needed for tradeable bottoms. Yet investor confidence remains fragile as [Federal Reserve officials appear deeply divided](https://www.investopedia.com/investors-just-endured-a-brutally-volatile-week-what-s-next-for-the-stock-market-spx-djia-comp-11854831) heading into next month's policy meeting, compounding uncertainty around valuations that now depend heavily on sustained AI monetization. The ongoing U.S.-Iran tensions add another layer of risk, though markets have thus far maintained their risk appetite despite the geopolitical backdrop.

socialHighlights.text: Dan Niles noted that 65% of his 17 key technical metrics signaled oversold conditions during the recent selloff, above the 48% threshold historically needed for a tradeable short-term bottom, while expecting volatility to decline from the VIX's spike to 50.

Yahoo Finance reported that the U.S. economy added just 57,000 jobs in June, well below economist expectations, though the unemployment rate dipped to 4.2%, giving investors mixed signals ahead of the Fed's next move.

MarketWatch highlighted that semiconductor stocks led S&P 500 laggards with SanDisk down 14.13%, Teradyne falling 13.63%, and KLA Corporation dropping 11.51%, underscoring the sector rotation away from AI chip plays.

sections.headline: Consumer Spending Defies Inflation Surge as Households Reshape Budgets
sections.summary: U.S. consumer spending rose 0.7% in May even as inflation accelerated to 4.1% annually—the fastest pace in three years and double the Federal Reserve's 2% target. While households continue to spend, particularly on necessities, changing behaviors reveal a deeper story of budget strain, with shoppers cutting discretionary items, trading down to private labels, and stockpiling during promotions.
sections.editorial:
The persistence of consumer spending amid elevated inflation presents a puzzling resilience that masks significant household adjustments. [Core PCE inflation hit 3.4%](https://www.retaildive.com/news/consumer-spending-rises-inflation-speeds-up-three-year-high/823861/), the highest reading since October 2023, yet consumers maintained their purchasing momentum with both income and spending rising 0.7%. This dynamic has kept the Federal Reserve in a holding pattern on rate decisions while [complicating the outlook for gold and other safe-haven assets](https://www.thestreet.com/investing/golds-rebound-has-a-problem-heres-what-happens-next). The spending figures, however, tell only part of the story—beneath the surface, households are executing strategic retreats across their budgets.

Consumer coping mechanisms reveal stress fractures in spending power that aggregate data obscures. [Nearly half of grocery shoppers have eliminated snacks and junk food](https://chainstoreage.com/survey-grocery-shoppers-remain-concerned-prices-could-keep-rising) while 39% reduced beef purchases, yet 68% still prioritize fresh groceries despite higher costs. The shift toward [private-label products and promotion-based stockpiling](https://www.jpmorgan.com/insights/global-research/economy/inflation-cost-of-living)—with 51% of shoppers timing purchases around sales—demonstrates calculated adaptation rather than unconstrained demand. Research from the [European Central Bank on Chinese households](https://insurancenewsnet.com/oarticle/researchers-from-european-central-bank-report-on-findings-in-economics-and-statistics-consumption-response-to-minimum-wages-evidence-from-chinese-households-economics-economics-and-statistics) found that lower-income families exhibit marginal propensities to consume near 100%, channeling additional income entirely into spending rather than savings—a pattern that may explain continued U.S. consumption despite affordability pressures. Meanwhile, contrasting signals from Europe, where [German inflation cooled to 2.4% and French inflation dropped to 2.0%](https://www.kitco.com/news/off-the-wire/2026-06-30/inflation-cools-biggest-euro-economies-easing-rate-hike-urgency), underscore the divergence in monetary policy trajectories and household purchasing environments across major economies.

socialHighlights.text: Axios reported that real per capita disposable income declined 1.4% year-over-year in April, marking the first consecutive negative readings since late 2023—a metric some analysts consider a powerful predictor of election outcomes.

Investopedia's analysis highlighted that consumers are cutting back on services like dining and travel while maintaining retail spending, supported by higher tax refunds, though persistent inflation could weaken spending for lower-income households later in 2026.

sections.topic: credit card rewards interest rates Fed
sections.headline: Credit Card Economics Face Turbulence as Rate Pressures Mount and Rewards Models Evolve
sections.summary: Credit card interest rates have surged to a record 23.37% average, driven by Federal Reserve policy shifts, while the rewards ecosystem faces potential disruption from both regulatory proposals and new gamified competitors. As consumer spending rises amid three-year-high inflation, the $15 billion annual redistribution from low-FICO to high-FICO cardholders through rewards programs draws renewed scrutiny.
sections.editorial:
The credit card industry is navigating a volatile landscape where traditional business models collide with macroeconomic headwinds and evolving consumer expectations. Average interest rates have climbed dramatically—from [16.43% in August 2020 to 23.37% today](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/credit-card-interest-rates-surging-how-to-lower-yours-now)—as the Federal Reserve's rate adjustments ripple through variable-rate products. With [80% of futures traders expecting another rate increase by year-end](https://www.retaildive.com/news/consumer-spending-rises-inflation-speeds-up-three-year-high/823861/), cardholders carrying balances face mounting costs. Meanwhile, premium offerings proliferate: [Chase's Sapphire Preferred now touts a 100,000-point welcome bonus](https://upgradedpoints.com/news/redeem-chase-sapphire-preferred-100k-bonus-first-class/), Amazon Prime rolls out [50-cent-per-gallon gas discounts](https://www.cnbc.com/select/amazon-offering-50-cents-off-gas/), and card issuers establish [exclusive lounges to compete for affluent customers](https://www.cnbc.com/video/2026/07/03/why-credit-card-lounges-are-popping-up-everywhere.html).

Yet this rewards arms race operates on a fundamentally regressive foundation. Federal Reserve research documents how [rewards programs redistribute $15.1 billion annually from low-FICO to high-FICO consumers](https://www.federalreserve.gov/econres/feds/files/2023007pap.pdf), with super-prime cardholders capturing disproportionate benefits while subprime customers subsidize through higher fees and interest. Proposed regulatory interventions—including [Trump administration discussions of interest rate caps](https://www.investopedia.com/trump-s-credit-card-interest-rate-cap-could-mean-diminished-rewards-points-and-miles-11883929)—could compress issuer margins and force "significant shifts in program economics," potentially yielding fewer cards with higher fees and diminished rewards. Simultaneously, challengers like [Coverd Card are introducing gamified, instant-feedback reward structures](https://thefintechtimes.com/coverd-card-launches-gamified-credit-rewards-to-disrupt-stagnant-consumer-payments/) designed to appeal to consumers weary of "opaque, delayed points portals." As [inflation pressures compound](https://www.retaildive.com/news/consumer-spending-rises-inflation-speeds-up-three-year-high/823861/) and rate normalization remains uncertain, the industry's decades-old playbook faces its most substantive reckoning in a generation.

socialHighlights.text: CBS News reports that credit card interest rates have reached an all-time high of 23.37%, up from 18.43% in August 2022, meaning cardholders financing purchases now pay significantly more in interest than just a few years ago.

The Federal Reserve Bank of Boston explains that most credit cards have variable rates tied to the prime rate, which closely follows the federal funds rate set by the Fed, creating direct transmission from monetary policy to consumer borrowing costs.

Federal Reserve research reveals that credit card reward programs redistribute $15.1 billion annually, with high-FICO consumers benefiting at the expense of low-FICO consumers through both cost and benefit margins.

sections.headline: Housing Market Shows Signs of Rebalancing as Sellers Cut Prices, Buyers Return Despite 6.5% Mortgage Rates
sections.summary: The U.S. housing market is exhibiting its most significant shift in years, with asking prices falling 2.5% year-over-year—the steepest decline since 2017—while pending sales rise for a seventh consecutive month. Despite mortgage rates stubbornly hovering around 6.5%, buyers are re-entering the market as sellers adopt more realistic pricing strategies and inventory conditions improve in key regions.
sections.editorial:
After months of gridlock, the American housing market appears to be finding equilibrium through an unusual combination of seller capitulation and buyer resilience. [Realtor.com data](https://nationalmortgageprofessional.com/news/home-sellers-lower-prices-while-buyers-return-realtorcom) reveals that asking prices dropped 2.5% year-over-year in June—the largest decline in nearly a decade—while pending sales climbed for a seventh straight month, rising 3.7% compared to last year. This price adjustment represents a fundamental shift from the seller-dominated dynamics that characterized much of the post-pandemic era. Remarkably, homes are no longer taking longer to sell than they did a year ago, suggesting the market has finally absorbed the shock of [elevated mortgage rates near 6.5%](https://finance.yahoo.com/personal-finance/mortgages/article/mortgage-rates-hover-near-65-as-other-housing-metrics-show-modest-improvement-mortgage-rates-today-july-2-2026-100000176.html).

The persistence of buyer activity despite punishing borrowing costs underscores a strategic adaptation in purchasing behavior. Joel Kan, deputy chief economist for the Mortgage Bankers Association, notes that purchase applications have exhibited year-over-year growth for nearly three months as buyers target markets with ample inventory and moderating price growth. Meanwhile, [TheStreet reports](https://www.thestreet.com/real-estate/americans-face-major-decision-with-mortgage-rate-news) that Americans face a critical decision point as the Federal Reserve holds rates steady at 3.5%-3.75% amid elevated inflation. The share of listings with price cuts has actually decreased to 18.8%—down 1.9 percentage points—suggesting that while sellers are pricing more competitively from the outset, they're avoiding the desperation discounting that would signal genuine distress. This calibrated repricing, combined with buyers who are "finding opportunities" according to the MBA, points to a market that's normalizing rather than collapsing, though affordability remains constrained by the reality that even small rate movements can dramatically alter monthly payment calculations for price-sensitive households.

socialHighlights.text: CNBC highlighted that mortgage rates surged to their highest levels since September, delivering a blow to the spring housing market and buyer sentiment.

Consumer Financial Protection Bureau research shows that rate increases alone have added $838 to monthly payments on a $400,000 loan—a 52% increase that significantly strains housing affordability even before considering home price appreciation.

U.S. Bank analysis emphasizes that mortgage rates continue to be the primary driver of housing activity because they directly affect monthly payments, with many households now budgeting around the payment first and home price second.
